zones.name: Architecture Docs
zones.description: Project documentation covering architecture decisions, analysis reports, and operational guides — entirely static Markdown with no import graph edges.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 19 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
19 documentation files with zero cohesion and coupling is expected — these are reference artifacts consumed by humans and AI context loaders, not imported by source modules
The docs/analysis/ subdirectory contains audit reports (signal-handling, memory risks, viewer audit) that suggest a recurring architectural review practice — valuable for onboarding

docs/architecture/ files describe memory architecture and level systems; keeping these current when the corresponding code changes requires discipline — a CI freshness check (like the one already added for architecture docs) helps enforce this
Zero cohesion and coupling are expected and correct for a documentation-only zone — no structural action needed.

The breadth of analysis docs (signal handling, memory risks, resource allocation, process lifecycle) reflects thorough architectural auditing; ensure each report links to the PRD item or commit that resolved the finding to close the feedback loop.

docs/decisions/ (ADRs) and docs/architecture/ together form a two-tier documentation system — confirming that new architectural decisions are recorded here before implementation would prevent undocumented drift.

architecture-docs zone has no import relationship with any other zone, but its content directly governs the rules enforced in hench-agent and web-viewer — a semantic dependency that is invisible to the call graph

Documentation-to-code semantic dependency: architecture-docs governs gateway rules and tier boundaries that are enforced in hench-agent (rex-gateway.ts) and web-viewer (domain-gateway.ts), but this relationship is invisible to the import graph. Drift between docs and implementation can only be caught by the CI freshness check, not by zone health metrics.

ENFORCEMENT.md at the monorepo root follows the standard GitHub CoC enforcement file convention but its co-location with CLAUDE.md and CODEX.md risks confusion with architectural enforcement documents; its scope (community conduct vs architecture) is not stated in the filename

Architecture analysis docs under docs/analysis/ capture findings but contain no structured 'Resolution' field linking each finding to the PRD item or commit that addressed it — resolved findings may be re-raised in future analysis passes without a closed-loop reference

Add a standardized 'Resolution' section to each docs/analysis/ audit document linking to the PRD item ID or commit SHA that addressed the finding. Without this link, resolved findings are indistinguishable from open ones in future analysis passes, causing repeat triage effort.

zones.name: CLI End-to-End Tests
zones.description: End-to-end test suite that exercises the n-dx orchestration CLI commands, delegation contracts, architecture policies, and error paths against real spawned processes.
insights: High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
22 files covering CLI contracts, delegation, dev mode, config, and architecture policy gives strong regression coverage for the orchestration tier where integration is otherwise hard to unit-test.
architecture-policy.test.js is strategically important — it machine-enforces the four-tier import hierarchy and gateway rules, effectively acting as a living architecture fitness function.
cli-arg-contracts.test.js and cli-contract.test.js may overlap in scope; periodically audit for duplicate coverage and consolidate to reduce maintenance burden.

Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirms the e2e suite is fully isolated from production code modules, relying only on CLI spawn behavior — this is the correct pattern for orchestration-tier tests.

architecture-policy.test.js enforcing gateway and tier-boundary rules at the e2e layer is a strong architectural safeguard; it should be run in CI before any merge to main to catch boundary violations early.

cli-delegation.test.js validates that orchestration scripts spawn sub-CLIs rather than importing them directly — this is the automated guard for the spawn-vs-gateway decision rule and must remain in the suite as packages evolve.

cli-e2e-tests validates spawn-based delegation at the process boundary but provides no coverage of the HTTP MCP transport layer — session lifecycle, Streamable HTTP compliance, and concurrent MCP request handling are exercised only through manual testing or not at all.

The architecture-policy test enforces import hierarchy statically via source analysis but cannot detect runtime violations such as a gateway that wraps rather than purely re-exports a function, silently changing call semantics without violating any import rule.

The MCP HTTP transport path (ndx start → /mcp/rex, /mcp/sourcevision endpoints) has no e2e test coverage; the stdio transport path is validated indirectly through delegation tests, creating an asymmetric risk as HTTP becomes the recommended transport.

architecture-policy.test.js and the cross-package-integration-tests zone together form complementary enforcement layers (static import rules + runtime wiring), but the gap between them — runtime gateway shape correctness — is uncovered by either zone.

447 internal calls across 22 files (~20 calls/file) indicates significant shared utility usage within the e2e suite; if shared helpers encapsulate CLI spawn logic, test isolation is preserved, but if they share mutable process-level state (e.g. environment variable patches), a single flaky test could corrupt the environment for all subsequent tests in the same run.

The architecture-policy test enforces gateway and tier-boundary rules via static source analysis but has no runtime assertion that gateway files contain only re-exports and no logic; a gateway that wraps a function with added behavior (e.g. logging, error transformation) passes all static checks while silently violating the re-export-only contract.

cli-e2e-tests validates that orchestration scripts spawn sub-CLIs rather than importing them directly, but there is no test verifying the reverse — that no package-tier module ever spawns or shell-execs an orchestration-tier script (cli.js, web.js, ci.js). Package code calling back into orchestration scripts would invert the four-tier hierarchy at the process level, invisible to import-graph analysis.

The spawn-exempt config.js exception — the only orchestration-tier script permitted to read package files directly rather than spawning — has no dedicated test case in cli-e2e-tests that validates it uses direct file I/O rather than spawning. The exception is prose-only in CLAUDE.md, meaning a refactor that accidentally converts config.js to spawn-based delegation would pass all tests while silently breaking cross-package atomic config reads.

No e2e test asserts that package-tier modules (rex, sourcevision, hench) never shell-exec or spawn orchestration-tier scripts (cli.js, web.js, ci.js). While import-graph enforcement catches module-import inversions, process-spawn inversions from packages back into orchestration scripts are undetectable by static analysis. Add a test in cli-e2e-tests that scans package src/ directories for shell-exec or spawn calls targeting the monorepo root scripts.

config.js is the only orchestration-tier script with a documented exception to the spawn-only rule (it reads package config files directly). This exception has no automated test — a future refactor converting config.js to spawn-based delegation would silently break cross-package atomic config reads without failing any test. Add a test case to cli-e2e-tests that exercises a cross-package config read and asserts the result is atomically consistent (e.g. both rex and hench config values appear in a single invocation output).

scheduler-startup.test.js and cross-package-contracts.test.js cover different failure modes (runtime wiring vs API shape) — consider whether they share test utilities that could be extracted to a shared fixture.
Zero coupling to other zones is expected and healthy for a test-only zone; it confirms tests depend on public APIs rather than internal implementation details.
With only 2 files this zone is thin; as the integration surface grows (e.g. hench↔rex, web↔rex) additional contract tests should land here rather than in e2e to keep fast feedback loops.
scheduler-startup.test.js validates the concurrency contract documented in CLAUDE.md; keep it in sync whenever the concurrency contract table is updated.

With only 2 files, this zone covers the rex→web and scheduler startup contracts but has no test for the hench→rex gateway — the most active cross-package runtime dependency in the system is entirely absent from the integration test surface.

The zone's zero coupling to production zones is correct architecture, but it also means the integration tests cannot detect silent gateway promotion (where a type-only re-export is accidentally made a runtime import) — only a runtime import-shape assertion would catch this class of regression.

No integration test covers the hench→rex gateway contract; hench is the primary autonomous consumer of rex at runtime, yet rex-gateway.ts re-export stability is unvalidated in this zone — API drift between rex releases would go undetected until a hench runtime failure.

The two-file zone is disproportionately thin relative to the four active cross-package boundaries (hench→rex, web→rex, web→sourcevision, cli.js→all packages); as each boundary evolves independently the integration test gap widens.

16 internal calls across 2 files (~8 calls per file) for test files is low relative to cli-e2e-tests (~20 calls per file), suggesting the integration tests rely minimally on shared utilities — this is appropriate for narrow contract tests but also means any future test that needs shared setup must introduce that infrastructure from scratch rather than building on an existing base.

The concurrency contract table in CLAUDE.md documents 9 unsafe command pairs, but scheduler-startup.test.js only validates server startup sequencing — no test exercises concurrent writes to .rex/prd.json or .sourcevision/ to verify data-corruption prevention; the contract is documentation-only with zero automated regression.

The zone name 'cross-package-integration-tests' uses a structural-prefix naming pattern while 'cli-e2e-tests' uses a source-prefix pattern — the two test zones apply different naming conventions, making the test zone namespace inconsistent and harder to extend predictably.

The two test zone naming conventions are inconsistent: 'cross-package-integration-tests' names by structural role while 'cli-e2e-tests' names by source package + test type. A new contributor adding a hench e2e test zone has no canonical pattern to follow. Standardize on one convention (e.g. '{scope}-{test-type}-tests') and document it in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md.

Two zones share the ID 'hench-agent' (155-file main zone and 14-file cross-package residual) — duplicate zone IDs cause any zone-lookup-by-ID to return unpredictable results and render per-zone health thresholds unreliable since both entries match the same key

Two distinct Louvain communities are both assigned the zone ID 'hench-agent': the 155-file main zone (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) and the 14-file cross-package residual (cohesion 0.33, coupling 0.67). Duplicate zone IDs make health metrics ambiguous — tooling that aggregates or thresholds by ID silently blends metrics from two structurally incompatible groups. The residual zone requires a distinct ID before its metrics can be acted upon.

suggestions.ts appearing in a residual zone with low cohesion implies its import connectivity to the rest of hench store layer is weaker than expected; if it is imported by fewer hench modules than other store files it may be partially orphaned or undercoupled relative to its declared domain purpose

Add a zone hint explicitly assigning packages/web/src/landing/index.html to web-dashboard. Its presence in a low-cohesion residual zone may indicate it is imported by fewer modules than expected for a store-layer file. High cohesion (1.0) with zero coupling reflects a well-isolated root layer that does not import from downstream packages
The presence of multiple orchestration entry points here enforces the architectural rule that cross-tier coordination happens at the root, not inside packages
Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0.0 confirm this zone is correctly scoped as the composition root with no upstream dependencies — ideal for an orchestration tier.

31 files span config, docs, orchestration scripts, and CI — consider verifying that no package-internal logic has leaked into root-level scripts over time, as orchestration scripts should only spawn child processes.

zones.name: Src
insights: 12 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
The provider-interface + provider-registry pattern establishes a solid open/closed extension point — new vendors can be added without modifying core client logic.
Token usage tracking is correctly co-located with the provider layer rather than in utilities, keeping billing concerns close to the transport boundary.
The bidirectional import with src-2 (3 imports src→src-2) is worth watching — ideally this foundational layer should have zero upward dependencies into the utility layer.
The 3 imports from this zone into src-2 (utilities) creates a mild coupling inversion — the foundation layer should not depend on the utility layer above it.
Cohesion of 0.6 is acceptable for a multi-provider abstraction; the provider registry and session files bind otherwise loosely related provider implementations together.

The 3 outgoing imports into llm-cli-utilities mean the foundational provider layer has a runtime dependency on the utility layer above it — any change to the utility API can break the provider core without a test catching it, since the test zone covers core but not utilities.

The 88 incoming calls from the test zone combined with 0 outgoing test calls to utilities creates a protection asymmetry: the core is well-tested but the utilities it depends on are not, so the 3 upward imports are doubly risky.

llm-provider-core imports 3 symbols from llm-cli-utilities while llm-provider-tests provides no coverage for those utility symbols — the 3 upward dependency edges are untested by the existing test suite.

The 4 outgoing external calls (to node built-ins or npm packages) combined with 88 incoming from tests suggests the core is correctly thin on external deps — the 3 utility imports stand out as the only inward-leaning coupling.

The 3 upward imports into llm-cli-utilities almost certainly originate from create-client.ts or a provider constructor needing runtime config (project dirs, auth tokens) — the dependency is driven by initialization concerns leaking into the transport layer rather than being injected at construction time.

The 12-entry-point surface amplifies the impact of the bidirectional coupling: every upward import from utilities has 12 possible re-export paths it could travel through, making it harder to trace which public symbols carry the layering violation.

12 exported entry points on a foundation layer that is imported by 23 edges from utilities and 25 from tests creates an oversized public contract — changes to any of the 12 exports can ripple into both consumer zones simultaneously. Reduce to 1–2 entry points aligned with the src/public.ts convention used across the monorepo.

create-client.ts and the provider constructors importing runtime config or auth from llm-cli-utilities indicates initialization concern leakage — providers should receive config values via constructor injection rather than pulling them from a higher-level utility layer, eliminating the 3 reverse imports structurally.

codex-cli-provider.ts encodes a vendor name in a file that sits alongside transport-named peers (api-provider, cli-provider) — the naming convention mixes vendor identity with transport strategy at the file level, making it unclear whether future vendors should be named by vendor (openai-cli-provider) or by transport (http-provider).

llm-types.ts is generically named in a zone scoped to provider concerns — if this file contains package-wide shared types (token structs, config shapes) rather than provider-only types, its placement in provider-core is the structural root cause of the 3 reverse imports: core holds types that utilities need, so utilities import core to get them rather than importing a neutral types zone.

provider-session.ts implies stateful session management at the transport layer, but auth credentials live in auth.ts in llm-cli-utilities with no visible contract between the two — session construction likely requires auth context, making the session↔auth coupling implicit and untraceable across zone boundaries.

Rename codex-cli-provider.ts to follow the transport-first naming pattern used by api-provider and cli-provider — e.g. codex-provider.ts or openai-cli-provider.ts depending on whether the file abstraction is vendor or transport. Mixing both in the filename breaks the zone's naming convention and will compound as more vendors are added.

Audit llm-types.ts to determine whether it holds provider-scoped or package-wide types. If it contains types shared with llm-cli-utilities (project dir shapes, token structs, provider config interfaces), move those types to a new zero-dependency types module — this is likely the direct cause of the 3 reverse imports and fixing the placement fixes the cycle structurally.

The 12 entry-point warning (src/0) is a heuristic threshold crossing but has no corroborating LLM finding. With a public.ts convention confirmed in global finding 2, the wide surface may be intentional delegation. Do not treat this as critical without additional evidence.

The 12 entry points from automated heuristic analysis (src/0) and the 'single public entry point' claim from LLM pass 1 (global/2) are in direct tension. Both cannot be simultaneously true at the intra-package zone level — either the src zone has one file imported from outside (supporting global/2) or twelve (supporting src/0). This discrepancy has not been reconciled in any subsequent pass.

Findings src/0 (12 entry points, automated heuristic) and global/2 (single public entry point pattern, LLM pass 1) contradict each other. The automated heuristic counts files in the src zone that have incoming import edges from other zones; if 12 such files exist, the src/public.ts single-entry-point convention is not being honored at the zone boundary level. Audit which of the 12 src zone files are imported directly by src-2 zone files and determine whether those imports should be consolidated to route through a single gateway or public.ts re-export.

Code inspection confirms api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, auth.ts, create-client.ts, and llm-client.ts all import from types.ts, llm-types.ts, provider-interface.ts, and config.ts. If those type/config files are classified into the src-2 zone by Louvain clustering, these are the edges producing the bidirectional coupling count. The imports themselves are architecturally correct (services depending on types), so the coupling metric reflects zone misclassification, not a logic error.

Audit the 12 files in the src zone that have incoming import edges from src-2. Cross-reference each against src/public.ts exports to determine whether the import is correctly surfaced through the package entrypoint or bypasses it. Priority files to check: types.ts, llm-types.ts, provider-interface.ts, and config.ts — all confirmed as direct imports from api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, auth.ts, and create-client.ts. If these type/config files are the source of all 12 entry points, the finding reflects zone boundary placement rather than an API surface violation.

src/0 (12 entry points, call-graph-derived) and global/2 (single entry-point pattern, LLM-inferred) describe different scopes: global/2 observes the public.ts convention's intent at the package boundary; src/0 counts intra-package cross-zone import edges, which are expected within a single package. Both can be simultaneously correct — the contradiction is terminological. global/2 should not be relied upon to disprove src/0.

Before treating the 12 entry-point count as a public API violation, confirm whether the edges are intra-package (src-2 files importing from src files within the same package) or cross-package (external consumers bypassing public.ts). Intra-package cross-zone imports are architectural zone concerns, not public API violations, and do not require remediation via public.ts consolidation. Only cross-package direct imports need to be routed through public.ts.

The 12 entry points in src/0 and global/2's single-public-entry-point observation are not contradictory — they operate at different scopes. src/0 counts intra-package cross-zone import edges (src-2 files importing src files within llm-client); global/2 describes the external package API surface via public.ts. Both can be simultaneously correct. The practical consequence: src/0 is an internal zone-boundary concern, not a public API violation — remediation targets zone boundary clarity (pinning or reclassification), not public.ts consolidation.

The 12 entry-point count (src/0) reflects intra-package cross-zone import edges — src-2 files importing directly from src files within the same package. This is an architectural zone-boundary concern, not a public API surface violation. External consumers are subject to the public.ts contract; the heuristic does not indicate that contract is broken. The finding and global/2 (single public entry point pattern) are both correct at different scopes: global/2 describes the external boundary; src/0 describes internal zone coupling.

Mixing UI formatting and credential handling in an undifferentiated file set means a security audit of auth.ts must also consider whether output.ts inadvertently logs credentials.

Establish a file-naming prefix or directory grouping convention that maps visibly to the zone name — e.g. group files under src/config/, src/auth/, src/cli/ subdirectories. The current flat structure with a zone name that does not appear in any filename makes zone membership invisible to contributors and tooling.

The combination of 42% file concentration, generic name, and being the destination of 23 upward import crossings makes this zone the highest-priority candidate for renaming and internal boundary documentation — not splitting, since global finding 3 confirms four zones is appropriate granularity.

Manually assign a domain-specific name to this zone (e.g. 'llm-cli-utilities') in the sourcevision configuration. web-dashboard's near-perfect cohesion despite 391 files suggests the Louvain algorithm is correctly recognizing the server↔viewer internal import density; periodically re-run sourcevision analyze to confirm this remains true as the package grows.

The import graph has exactly one directed cross-zone production edge in this batch: hench-agent → rex (via rex-gateway.ts). All other inter-zone relationships are either spawn-based (monorepo-root → all), file-I/O-based (all → rex-runtime-data), or semantic (architecture-docs → all). This means zone coupling metrics capture only a fraction of real inter-zone dependencies in this monorepo.

The gateway pattern (rex-gateway.ts in hench, rex-gateway.ts + domain-gateway.ts in web) converts what would be diffuse many-to-many cross-zone import edges into a single auditable edge per upstream zone — this is why hench-agent coupling reads as 0.0 despite genuine runtime dependency on rex.

Three distinct dependency modalities exist across zones: (1) module imports mediated by gateways (hench→rex, web→rex, web→sourcevision), (2) spawn-based process invocation (monorepo-root→all), and (3) shared file I/O (hench+web→rex-runtime-data). Only modality (1) is visible to zone metrics — architectural health audits must explicitly account for all three.

The only zone with coupling above 0.1 in the entire batch is the cross-package residual zone (0.67), confirming that the gateway pattern and spawn-only orchestration rule together hold coupling to near-zero across all legitimate zone boundaries. The residual zone stands out as a clear outlier requiring remediation.

web-viewer (391 files, cohesion 0.98) and hench-agent (155 files, cohesion 1.0) have zero direct import relationship with each other — they interact only through rex-runtime-data (disk) and the web server's gateway modules. This confirms the execution-tier isolation is holding: hench and the viewer never share in-process state.

The heaviest call-graph traffic is entirely within-package (574, 243, 187, 104, 88 calls between unit and source sub-zones), while the only inter-package boundary visible is web-dashboard's 21 outgoing calls — this compression ratio validates that the gateway pattern is successfully limiting cross-package API surface to a near-zero footprint.

The three visible zones form a testing complement to the production system, but there is no zone dedicated to hench agent e2e behavior — hench's autonomous loop (tool dispatch, multi-turn conversation, run recording) is tested only within the hench package's own unit/integration tests, leaving the agent←→orchestrator boundary unverified at the system level.

All three zones have coupling at or below 0.02 and cohesion at or above 0.98, while the 'web' residual zone scores 0.33 cohesion — the stark contrast makes the residual zone the single most actionable structural issue visible in this batch and a reliable leading indicator of zone-assignment drift in the web package.

The test zones' perfect isolation (0 coupling to production zones) combined with web-dashboard's 0 incoming calls means no production zone is structurally entangled with tests — this is the correct topology and should be enforced by the architecture-policy test to prevent accidental test-helper imports from leaking into production bundles.

The monorepo uses three structurally distinct inter-zone coupling mechanisms — module imports (gateway-mediated), process spawning (orchestration tier), and shared file I/O (rex-runtime-data) — but zone cohesion/coupling metrics only measure the first. Architectural health reports should label zero-coupling zones as 'import-decoupled' rather than 'fully decoupled' to avoid masking spawn and file-I/O dependencies.

Every legitimate cross-zone production import in this batch flows through an explicit gateway module. The absence of any gateway-bypassing import edges (i.e., no direct leaf-to-leaf cross-zone imports) indicates the gateway pattern is being consistently enforced across all consuming zones.

The call-graph compression ratio (thousands of within-package calls vs 21 cross-package calls from the composition root) is a quantitative validation that the gateway + spawn-only architecture is working; tracking this ratio over time is a low-cost architectural fitness metric.

No zone provides e2e coverage of the hench autonomous agent loop in the context of a real rex PRD and live sourcevision output — the system's most complex integration path (ndx work end-to-end) has no automated regression test at the zone level.

rex-runtime-data functions as a shared-state bus for hench-agent, web-viewer, and monorepo-root via disk I/O. Concurrent writes from multiple zones to the same files (prd.json, execution-log.jsonl) represent inter-zone coupling that is architecturally undocumented at the zone level and cannot be detected by import-graph analysis. A zone-level concurrency contract document would make this coupling explicit.

The two test zones (cli-e2e-tests, cross-package-integration-tests) divide responsibility along process boundary vs API contract lines, but the hench→rex runtime contract and the MCP HTTP session protocol are orphaned — neither zone claims them, leaving two active integration surfaces with zero automated coverage.

The three inter-zone dependency modalities have asymmetric protection: module imports are guarded by the gateway pattern and CI import tests, spawn-based calls are guarded by the orchestration-tier spawn-only rule, but file I/O writes to rex-runtime-data have no protective pattern whatsoever — they are documented only in CLAUDE.md prose. This asymmetry makes the file I/O modality the weakest link in the architectural safety net.

Zone ID uniqueness is not enforced by the zone detection pipeline after Louvain partitioning. The current 'hench-agent' duplicate is a known artifact, but the absence of a post-processing uniqueness constraint means future analysis runs can silently introduce new collisions that corrupt aggregated health dashboards without any diagnostic signal.

The call graph entry 'packages-rex:core → packages-rex:unit' (104 calls) is the only production-to-test-named-zone edge in the visible call graph data; if 'unit' here refers to a test zone rather than a source zone, this represents a critical boundary inversion where production rex core code imports from test infrastructure — this must be verified and, if confirmed, treated as a critical anti-pattern.

The sourcevision internal call graph shows a bidirectional relationship: analyzers-3 → unit (574 calls) and unit → analyzers (187 calls), totalling 761 bidirectional calls; this mutual dependency within sourcevision's internal zones suggests either a shared utility layer mediating both directions (acceptable) or a genuine circular dependency (critical) — neither scenario is visible in the zone coupling metric, making it an invisible architectural risk.

The 'packages-rex:unit-analyze → packages-rex:unit-cli' call graph edge (243 calls) suggests rex's analyze-domain zone is coupled to its CLI-layer zone; within a single package this may be acceptable if 'unit-cli' is a test-only zone, but if it contains CLI implementation modules, analyze domain code depending on CLI code violates the domain/CLI layering that the four-tier hierarchy implies at the intra-package level.

The three execution-tier entry points (cli.js, web.js, ci.js) and config.js share nine documented unsafe command pairs but there is no machine-enforced mutex, lock file, or PID-based guard preventing concurrent invocation; the concurrency contract is entirely convention-based, making it invisible to new contributors and unenforceable in automated pipelines that parallelize steps.

The overall test-to-production file ratio is approximately 24 test files (cli-e2e-tests + cross-package-integration-tests) to 391+ production files in web-dashboard alone; this ratio is structurally low for the composition root of the system, where integration failures are most likely and most costly to debug.

No zone in the visible topology owns the MCP HTTP session lifecycle contract — session creation, Mcp-Session-Id header propagation, and session teardown behavior are neither documented as a zone responsibility nor covered by any test zone; as HTTP transport becomes the recommended path, this orphaned contract becomes an increasing reliability risk.

File I/O coupling to rex-runtime-data is the only inter-zone dependency modality with no protective pattern: imports have gateways + CI tests, spawns have the orchestration-tier rule, but concurrent disk writes to prd.json and execution-log.jsonl have only prose documentation in CLAUDE.md. This gap will become the most likely source of data-corruption bugs as the number of concurrent writers grows.

Zone IDs are not validated for uniqueness after Louvain partitioning. The current 'hench-agent' duplicate is a known artifact but the absence of a post-processing uniqueness check means future analysis runs could silently introduce additional collisions that degrade zone health reporting without any warning.

The 'packages-rex:core → packages-rex:unit' call graph edge (104 calls) potentially represents production source code importing from a test-named zone; if confirmed, production code depending on test infrastructure is a critical boundary inversion that would prevent tree-shaking, pollute production bundles, and make the test/source boundary unreliable across the entire rex package.

Bidirectional call graph between sourcevision internal zones (analyzers-3 ↔ unit: 761 combined calls) is undetectable by zone coupling metrics because both zones are within the same package boundary; a genuine circular dependency at this scale would prevent independent evolution of the analyzer and unit layers and is invisible to all current automated checks.

The four orchestration entry points share mutable disk state (.rex/prd.json, .sourcevision/) with no file-lock mechanism; any CI pipeline that parallelizes steps (e.g. running ndx ci and ndx work concurrently) will silently corrupt shared state with no error surfaced — the risk is highest in PR-triggered CI where multiple job steps may overlap.

Gateway file size is unbounded: as web-dashboard grows, rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts accumulate re-exports with no enforced limit; a gateway that grows to hundreds of re-exports becomes as difficult to audit as a direct many-to-many import graph, defeating the single-gateway pattern's auditability goal.

The packages-rex:core → packages-rex:unit call graph edge (104 calls) has been flagged across multiple enrichment passes as a potential critical boundary inversion but has no assigned verification action. After 4 passes without resolution, a targeted grep for import statements in packages/rex/src/core/ pointing to test-named directories is overdue — the correct disposition (critical boundary inversion vs misleadingly-named source zone) determines whether rex's entire domain layer is structurally compromised.

Zone health data uses semantic IDs (hench-agent, rex-runtime-data) while call graph data uses path-based IDs (packages-rex:core, packages-sourcevision:unit). Cross-referencing between health metrics and call graph edges requires manual name-mapping; a canonical zone-registry mapping semantic IDs to file path prefixes would make this relationship machine-readable and allow automated cross-zone analysis.

The bidirectional sourcevision internal relationship (packages-sourcevision:analyzers-3 ↔ packages-sourcevision:unit, 761 combined calls) has been identified as an invisible risk across multiple passes but has no assigned triage action. A concrete next step — confirm shared-utility mediation (acceptable) or circular dependency (critical) — should be tracked as a PRD item before the next major sourcevision refactor.

The ndx usage command is listed in CLAUDE.md but its implementing package is undocumented. Usage analytics could live in rex (PRD operation), web (dashboard feature), or hench (agent telemetry). Ambiguous command ownership in cli.js routing is a hidden maintenance risk when packages are refactored independently.

Gateway minimality enforcement covers the rex-gateway.ts case (8 functions, no consumer-count check) but the same gap applies to web's rex-gateway.ts and domain-gateway.ts which are growing alongside the web-dashboard. A shared gateway-coverage test that maps each re-exported symbol to its import count inside the consuming package — and fails if count is zero — would enforce minimality across all gateways simultaneously rather than requiring per-gateway manual audits.

The two incompatible zone ID naming schemes (semantic vs path-based) across zone health and call graph outputs reflect two independent analysis pipelines with no shared registry. As the number of zones grows, the manual mapping burden compounds: a zone renamed in one pipeline will silently break cross-pipeline lookups while appearing correct in each pipeline individually.

The re-export-only gateway contract has asymmetric enforcement: web's gateways are covered by domain-isolation.test.js, but hench's rex-gateway.ts has no equivalent — enforcement is package-scoped rather than monorepo-wide, creating a silent gap for any future gateway added to hench or a new execution-tier package.

Zone-to-package name mapping is non-trivial: two of the three largest zones use names that diverge from their parent package directory (web→web-dashboard, hench→hench-agent). This divergence is undocumented as a convention, meaning zone IDs cannot be derived algorithmically from package names, and any tooling that needs to correlate them must maintain an explicit mapping table.

The four-tier hierarchy is enforced top-down (orchestration must not import packages) but not bottom-up (packages must not spawn orchestration scripts). The spawn direction of the dependency rule has no machine-enforced test, leaving a class of hierarchy inversions detectable only by manual code review.

The docs zone (cohesion 0, coupling 0) and .rex zone (cohesion 0, coupling 0) both score zero on all metrics because they contain no TypeScript import edges — but they appear in the same health dashboards as source zones, silently dragging average cohesion down and making aggregate health metrics misleading. A zone-type discriminator (source | data | docs | test) would allow dashboards to report meaningful averages over source-only zones.

Across all zones, the only cross-zone production dependency modality that is both visible to metrics AND has a protective pattern (gateway + CI test) is module imports. Spawn-based and file-I/O-based dependencies are each protected by only one layer (rule vs nothing), creating a three-tier protection asymmetry that is nowhere documented as an explicit architectural risk gradient.

Create a triage PRD item to definitively resolve the packages-rex:core → packages-rex:unit call graph edge (104 calls). Run grep for import statements in packages/rex/src/core/ referencing test directories. If production→test imports are confirmed, this is a critical boundary inversion that pollutes production bundles and must be fixed before further rex domain refactoring. If 'unit' is a source-layer zone with a misleading name, rename it to remove the ambiguity.

Introduce a canonical zone registry (a machine-readable JSON or YAML file) that maps each semantic zone ID to its file path prefix. This would allow automated cross-referencing between zone health metrics (which use semantic IDs) and call graph data (which uses path-based IDs), eliminating the current manual name-mapping requirement for cross-pipeline analysis.

Document the implementing package for the ndx usage command in CLAUDE.md alongside the command entry. Undocumented command ownership creates ambiguity when packages are independently refactored and makes it impossible to locate the relevant source without tracing cli.js spawn logic.

Extend the domain-isolation test to enforce gateway minimality across all gateway files (hench/src/prd/rex-gateway.ts, web/src/server/rex-gateway.ts, web/src/server/domain-gateway.ts). For each re-exported symbol, assert that at least one consumer import exists inside the consuming package. Zero-consumer re-exports should fail the test, ensuring gateways are pruned as the dependency surface shrinks.

Create a PRD item to triage the bidirectional sourcevision internal call graph relationship (packages-sourcevision:analyzers-3 ↔ packages-sourcevision:unit, 761 combined calls). Determine whether calls flow through a shared utility module (acceptable) or represent a genuine circular dependency (critical requiring immediate decomposition). Leaving this unresolved after 4 analysis passes blocks confident architectural assessment of the sourcevision package.

Gateway enforcement is package-scoped: domain-isolation.test.js covers web's gateways, but no equivalent test exists for hench's rex-gateway.ts. Extend architecture-policy.test.js or create a monorepo-level gateway-isolation test that enumerates all files matching the gateway naming pattern (*-gateway.ts) across all packages and asserts each contains only re-export statements, making enforcement uniform and automatically extending to future gateways.

Zone IDs diverge from package directory names (web→web-dashboard, hench→hench-agent) with no documented mapping convention. Add an explicit zone-to-package mapping table to PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md and validate it in the zone detection pipeline to prevent diagnostic tooling from treating zone IDs and package names as interchangeable identifiers.

The docs and .rex zones score cohesion 0 and coupling 0 because they contain no TypeScript import edges, but they appear alongside source zones in health dashboards. Introduce a zone-type field (source | data | docs | test) in the zone schema and filter non-source zones from cohesion/coupling aggregate calculations to prevent zero-metric zones from distorting monorepo-level health scores.

The four-tier hierarchy spawn rule is enforced only in the downward direction (orchestration must not import packages) by cli-delegation.test.js. The upward direction (packages must not spawn orchestration scripts) has no automated enforcement. Add a monorepo-level test that scans all package src/ files for child_process spawn or exec calls targeting cli.js, web.js, ci.js, or config.js by name.

web-dashboard has 21 outgoing cross-zone calls but 0 incoming, confirming it is the composition root; any future zone that imports from web-dashboard would invert this pattern and should be treated as a critical boundary violation.

Two distinct Louvain communities are both assigned the zone ID 'hench-agent': the 155-file main zone (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0) and the 14-file cross-package residual (cohesion 0.33, coupling 0.67). The residual zone requires a distinct ID before its metrics can be acted upon.

The architecture-policy e2e test verifies the four-tier import hierarchy generally, but config.js's specific exception boundary is untested — a package module import added to config.js would pass CI undetected.

This is a missing abstraction layer for a shared mutable resource with multiple concurrent writers.

No automated assertion verifies that all 21 outgoing cross-zone calls flow through the two gateway files and none bypass them via direct leaf-to-leaf imports; a new developer adding a cross-package import in a non-gateway file would pass all existing tests undetected until sourcevision re-analysis.
